PURPOSE

The Investigation Training Précis are maintained by the Investigative Standards Unit. The précis have been written to offer guidance and assistance to all members of the Western Australian Police Force (WA Police Force) in relation to police investigations.

The précis are written to highlight the theory and methodology underpinning ‘Criminal Investigations’ and are not intended to be referenced as Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

Should information regarding SOPs be sought then readers should refer to the ‘Investigation Standards’ link located on the WA Police Force computer desktop.

The précis are an ‘all of agency’ resource but readers should be aware they are specifically written for Investigator Training.

INTRODUCTION

A statement is the product of the interview process. Investigators should remember when interviewing a witness the primary objective is to obtain a comprehensive account with the secondary objective being to record the account in a statement. A good statement will only ever be possible by conducting a quality interview utilising recognised ‘Investigative Interviewing’1 techniques. Conversely, a poor interview will result in a poor quality statement.

For further information, refer to the ‘Investigative Standards’ website under the ‘Investigative Interviewing’ tab.

Additionally a witness statement can be used by the investigation team to better inform investigators to plan and strategise investigative actions. This subject addresses the methodology that should be utilised by investigators to prepare, formulate and structure quality statements to best reflect the potential evidence to be provided by a witness.

Statements are a key method of recording the information and evidence obtained as a result of witness related investigative actions under the Five Key Investigative Strategies (The5KIS)2.

sections.h:
A statement is a guide for the prosecution and defence detailing the oral testimony a witness may give. It captures all of the potentially relevant oral testimony that a witness will give at trial. The purpose of the witness statement is to set out the evidence of the witness prior to the trial commencing so that all parties involved in the trial have an appreciation of the situation. Therefore the statement must fully reflect all the potential evidence that the witness can (or cannot) provide to the court. This will ensure any decision to conduct the trial or to enter a plea of guilty will be fully informed and based on the totality of the available evidence.

1 Western Australia Police Force, Investigative Interviewing, Witness Manual (Perth: WaPol 2018) 2 Western Australia Police Force, WA Police, Investigation Doctrine (Perth: WaPol 2017) The construction of a witness statement is a relatively simple process but there are many nuances that need to be adhered to, in order for the finished product to form a professional document that may be presented before the court.

The WA Police Investigation Doctrine identifies five categories of witnesses (see below).

Witnesses come in many forms and each is as important as the other during the trial process. It is the testimony of all witnesses as a collective that reconstruct the offence and subsequent investigation within the confines of the court. Therefore, investigators should maintain professional standards for all statements prepared from all witnesses. It is possible a witness may fall into a number of categories dependent on the circumstances of the offence and this includes police officers. Where this occurs investigators should ensure the statement meets the expectations of each respective category.

Victim

The content of the victim’s statement will depend on the circumstances of the offence(s) and may include proof of facts such as legal entity, age, legal status, consent and/or all of the elements. Investigators should ensure they are fully conversant with the elements of the offence(s) under investigation and where required are addressed in the statement accordingly.

Eye Witnesses

Eyewitness testimony is generally presumed to be the best type of evidence. This is typically because the witness can give evidence of what they saw, heard, smelt, touched or tasted whilst the offence was actually occurring.

There have been numerous studies into witnesses. One study involved an experiment in which subjects acted as jurors in a criminal case. Jurors heard a description of a robbery- murder, then a prosecution argument, then an argument for the defence. Some jurors heard only circumstantial evidence, others heard from a clerk who claimed to identify the defendant. In the first case, 18% percent found the defendant guilty, but in the second, 72% found the defendant guilty3.

Although studies indicate that eye witness testimony carries a lot of weight with jurors, eye witness testimony is often flawed, this can occur because of a person's faulty observation and recollection, a person's bias or due to the witness lying. If several people witness a crime it is probative to look for similarities in their collective descriptions to substantiate the facts of an event, keeping in mind the contrasts of individual descriptions. This illustrates the point that investigators should not stop the search for witnesses just because they have identified one eye witness.

3 Bell, B.E., & Loftus, E.F. Degree of detail of eyewitness testimony and mock juror judgements. Journal of Applied Social Psychology, (1988). 18(14), 1171-1192

Other Significant Witnesses

‘Other Significant Witness’ is a person who has observed (by one or more of the five senses) events which are relevant before or after the actual offence. Examples include the clothing worn by a suspect, observing the suspect to leave a house after they have committed an offence, observing a suspect to play with a knife before the offence, police officer interviewing the suspect etc. Therefore it is important the witness statement reflects how they observed these events but also articulates that they did not see the actual offence.

Expert Witnesses

Opinions, inferences and beliefs of ordinary individuals are irrelevant in a court of law. The opinions of experts are admissible if it is based on their area of expertise. An expert witness or professional witness is a person who, by virtue of their education, training, skill, or experience, is believed to have knowledge in a particular subject beyond that of the average person. It is considered this knowledge is sufficient that others may legally rely upon the witness's specialised opinion about evidence or a fact in issue within the scope of their expertise and this is referred to as expert opinion.

Typically, experts are relied upon for opinions on cause of death, severity of injury, degree of insanity, comparisons of items, scientific experiments and the like. The expert has a heavy responsibility, especially in trials.

It is for these reasons that investigators should ensure that the expert witness’s statement clearly details:

1. The qualifications and experience of the person making the statement.

2. The relevance of the person’s qualification or experience to the fact in issue.

3. The processes used to establish the findings.

4. What the person’s expert opinion is in relation to the fact in issue.

General Witnesses

‘General Witness’ is a person who provides relevant evidence with regard to continuity and/or procedure. They may also give evidence of background information which assists with building a context for the offence(s). It is important the witness statement clearly reflects the role of the witness or articulates they are providing background information only. This type of evidence is often admissible under ‘Res Gestae’ the principle of completeness. An example of a general witness is a bank manager simply providing documents to police to support the commission of an offence.

• Conduct the interview in accordance with the Investigative Interviewing

methodology4. Notes should be taken during this phase.

• Complete a draft of the statement (handwritten or electronic).

• Check accuracy of statement content with interviewee and notes taken during the

interview phase.

• Peer review for evidentiary content and grammatical accuracy.

• Complete final draft and have the interviewee read it. Any changes requested by the

interviewee must be amended accordingly.

• Interviewee signs, times and dates each page (adoption) as does the officer

witnessing the signature.

Any variation between a statement and the testimony given by the witness will invariably result in cross-examination by the defence and it may, in some circumstances, affect the witness’s credibility.

The statement should read in a chronological order, this is because the testimony of the witness will in most cases be chronological. A jury will also be able to follow a chronological story better than one that jumps from event to event and back again.

Descriptions play a big part in the identification of an accused or other persons involved in an offence. All descriptions in a statement need to be as detailed as the witness can recall.

This will make any subsequent formal identification more credible, reduce faulty identifications and subject the evidence to less scrutiny by the court.

Description is not restricted to physical description, but includes the clothing described by the witness. Investigators are reminded that in the case of a sexual offence, a detailed description of the victims clothing is essential.

Hearsay Evidence

Hearsay evidence is a complex matter and will inevitably result in detailed legal argument.

Investigators are reminded that hearsay evidence should not be included in the statement unless it falls within the category of exceptions to the Hearsay Rules. Hearsay evidence is evidence of a statement made other than by a witness, who was present at the scene or incident, who perceived what occurred by one of his five senses – i.e. It is evidence of what someone else did or noticed.

sections.t: Content and Style of A Statement
sections.h:
The information contained within a statement will result from the interview with the witness and the application of Investigative Interviewing techniques will ensure that all relevant information is captured.

Special Circumstances

It is not the intent of this subject to

For offences where medical information is relevant,

regurgitate that which is supplied additional documents will be required such as:

within the subject of investigative • Authority to release medical information.

interviewing, however there are • Request for medical report.

several points that need to be raised. • Example doctors statement.

A witness statement must contain truthful and reliable evidence, according to the clear recollection and personal experience of the witness. Its contents should tell the prosecutor and defence what the witness recalls detailed in distinguishable topic areas (paragraphs).

The topic areas will assist the court to explore these topic areas in a logical manner and avoid confusing the witness evidence.

A witness statement is a story told by the witness about a series of events that the witness experienced in the past. For the most part these events can be described in the words of the witness by using reference to time, place and the experience.

Each event is part of the witness's personal experience. The description of the event should be what the witness saw, said, heard or did. When another person forms part of the experience of the witness then the description of the event should refer to what that other was heard to say or seen to do6. The rules of hearsay need to be considered when the conversation is relayed.

A witness statement that is prepared in this way becomes a written version of what otherwise might be the script for a film of the described events. If an event cannot be imagined as image or sound then it is likely that the witness statement is not in the correct form.

The witness should be told by the interviewer to only include in their statement what they recollect and know to be true. That knowledge comes from recollection of personal experience of events and the witness should be confident about that recollection. The witness should not speculate or guess. If they are not confident about whether an event occurred then this should be detailed in the statement i.e. “John came over Friday night. I’m not sure what time it was or if he came with anyone, but I know it was Friday night because I was watching Friday Night Football”.

Correct Tense

Most evidence concerns events that occurred in the past. Witness statements should therefore use past and not present tense. The problem of incorrect use of present tense often arises when a witness describes a scene, or talks about a relationship between things, 6 Paper was presented on 11 September 2002 at an advocacy course conducted by the Supreme Court of Western Australia and Murdoch University

system, practice, or state of affairs. The use of present tense disguises the true evidence about the state of affairs at the relevant time.

Formatting Conversation

Frequently witness statements set out conclusions about the nature or the subject of what was said rather than state the words used. This is not evidence. Usually it is a conclusion about or evaluation of personal experience that has not been set out in the statement. The specified substance of the words spoken constitutes the evidence.

When including conversation into a statement then

Formatting speech (I said / He said)

the “I said. He said.” format must be used.

I said, “What are you doing with that knife” Conversation that is not relevant can be paraphrased such as:

He said, “I just found it”

The above example is sufficient when the conversation is clearly between yourself and another person that is easily identifiable and cannot be confused with any other person.

Use Active Voice

Most sentences should identify the witness and if another person is involved should identify that person. The sentence should then say what the relevant individuals saw, said, heard or did. The witness may however be revealed by the context provided by the statement8.

Avoid Passive Voice

Passive expressions that simply describe an event without identifying the individuals concerned should be avoided. For example "The side of the house was where the man came from" does not reveal anything about what the witness experienced. The sentence should reveal that the witness saw the man come from the side of the house. Unless it is clear from the context the form should be "I saw the man walk from the side of the house"9.

Avoid Collective Expressions

The individual experience of the witness is usually relevant and not some form of collective or corporate experience. The witness should talk about what ‘I’ saw, did, said or heard rather than what ‘we’ or some corporate entity saw, did, said or heard10.

Likewise where another individual said or did something the individual should be identified and not ‘they’ or the name of some corporate or collective entity. Corporations do not talk or act, only people do and hence they must be identified. A sentence that links conduct to a corporation is a conclusion about whom the individual represented rather than a statement about what happened11.

It is rarely helpful or admissible to state in a witness statement what ‘we agreed’ or what ‘we discussed’. These are expressions of conclusions from words spoken that have not been stated12.

Avoid the State of Mind

In most cases the state of mind of the witness is not relevant. It may be relevant where an allegation is made that there was a common intention in a contractual context that was not correctly recorded in the final written contract and a claim is made for rectification. It might also be relevant in the context of foreseeability and the actual knowledge of the defendant when an allegation of negligence is made. In most cases however the state of mind of the witness is not relevant. It is not a description of the personal experience of the witness. It is not what the witness saw, said, did or heard13.

There are numerous expressions that reveal an irrelevant state of mind and should not be included in witness statements. They include ‘I understood’, ‘I thought’ or ‘I intended’.

The same applies to conversations. ‘When I said to her [words spoken] I meant ...’ and like forms should not be used. Again, interpretation usually is not a description of what the witness saw, said, heard or did14.

A witness statement must contain truthful and reliable evidence, according to the clear recollection and personal experience of the witness. Its contents should tell the prosecutor and defence what the witness recalls detailed in distinguishable topic areas (paragraphs). The topic areas will assist the court to explore these topic areas in a logical manner and avoid confusing the witness evidence. A witness statement is a story told by the witness about a series of events that the witness experienced in the past. For the most part these events can be described in the words of the witness by using reference to time, place and the experience. Each event is part of the witness's personal experience. The description of the event should be what the witness saw, said, heard or did. When another person forms part of the experience of the witness then the description of the event should refer to what that other was heard to say or seen to do6. The rules of hearsay need to be considered when the conversation is relayed. A witness statement that is prepared in this way becomes a written version of what otherwise might be the script for a film of the described events. If an event cannot be imagined as image or sound then it is likely that the witness statement is not in the correct form. sections.h:
The WA Police Force have a standard format for the construction of statements16. All statements must be prepared on the approved template.

Statements are to be compiled using the right half of the page only, as this allows space for the prosecutor to enter extensive notes on the left half.

Computer generated statements must be constructed using 12 point ‘ARIAL’ font, line spacing should be formatted at 1.5 and each paragraph is to be numbered. Margins at the top and bottom of the document are to 3 centimetres. Pages are to be numbered.

The name of the witness should be at the top of each page, the first page should read:

(Witnesses’ Name)

States.

Subsequent pages should read:

Statement of (Witnesses’ Name) continued.

Statement Introduction

The first paragraph of the witness’s statement should include a brief introduction. This introduction should contain the witness’s age, their occupation and the suburb they live in.

The witness’s actual address should not be included on the statement, unless the address is an evidentiary factor such as in the case of a burglary where an element of the offence is ‘enters a place of another’17 and as such the address becomes a fact that must be proved.

A police statement will also include an introduction. Traditionally police statements only included rank and work location, however with the introduction of educational qualifications and in line with the professionalising of investigators it is appropriate to include the investigators’ qualifications in the introductions.

Police statements should also include the Example Statement Introductions Witness Statement

16 Criminal Code Act, Compilation Act 1913 (WA).

Body of the Statement

The statement should always be written in the first-person. First-person is a narrative mode in which a story is narrated by only one character that explicitly refers to him or herself using words and phrases involving ‘I’ (referred to as the first-person singular). This style of writing ensures that the witness only indicates the things that they have experienced with one of their five senses.

The statement should be constructed using concise paragraphs to detail the points which are relevant to the matter under investigation. The use of concise paragraphs does not mean a reduction in the detail, it simply means that long expansive pieces of information should be structured clear sub-events.

Paragraphs should not be split over two pages, this makes the statement look unprofessional and in some circumstances could lead to allegations that part of the paragraph had been changed to alter the context.

Most statements will at some point refer to a time and this should be formatted in the 12 hour clock. The 24 hour clock format should not be used. Juries comprise members of the public and as such many will not be familiar with the 24 hour clock format. Confusion on behalf of the jury regarding times may have a monumental impact on the trial.

sections.h:
Throughout many statements there will be a need to include marginal notes to notify the prosecutor of the introduction of an exhibit or the identification of the accused.

Exhibit

If the witness makes reference to any ‘real evidence’18 in their statement the word ‘EXHIBIT’ is to be placed on the left-hand side of the page opposite the place or places in the statement where a reference is made to the exhibit. This does not only occur when the witness is tendering an exhibit but should occur at any point the first time an exhibit is referred to, for each individual item. This will prompt the prosecutor to show the exhibit to the witness and have it either tendered or marked for identification.

Marked for identification is a process where an exhibit is shown to witnesses involved in the trial and the witness indicates that is the exhibit to which they are referring, the exhibit is not tendered in evidence until the chain of custody is complete and the witness that had last possession of the exhibit gives evidence. The statement should include a detailed description of the exhibit including, if applicable serial numbers and model numbers. A minimal description of the exhibit could lead to confusion or legal challenge during the trial.

The most detailed description a witness can give for every exhibit is important.

18 See Investigation Precis, Physical Material Management.(Perth: WaPol 2017) The statement must also address what interaction the witness had with the exhibit, such as how the witness came to be in possession of the exhibit and what was done with it at the completion of dealing with it. This information will assist in constructing the chain of custody.

A document that is to be tendered through a witness should be explained by reference to the personal experience of the witness. The relevance of the document needs to be explained to ensure that’s it importance is conveyed to the jury.

Identify

Insert ‘IDENTIFY’on the left-hand side of the page opposite the place or places in the statement where a reference is made to an offender that has been categorically identified by name. This does not include when a witness that sees an offender and the offender is subsequently identified.

Example of Extract from Exhibits Officer Statement

Role Description

…The role of the Exhibits Officer is to receive and number each exhibit seized, record a description of the item, the details of the locating officer and where the item was located.

Property Example

During the search I was handed a black handled knife, the details of which were recorded onto Exhibit Log B12345 as item BWJ1.

… later I conveyed the item to the office and recorded it onto the police IMS system.

…The item was then secured in the property store.

Drug Example

During the search I was handed a clear plastic bag which contained a crystal like substance. I placed it into security movement envelope W0012345 and recorded it onto Exhibit Log number B12345 as item

BWJ1.

…I later attended the office where I recorded it onto the police Information Management System (IMS). I also opened Security Movement Envelope W0012345 and weighed the contents. I replaced the contents into Security Movement Envelope W0012346. I placed the Security Movement Envelope into the Secure Drug Storage Area.

…On the 01/01/2010 I conveyed Security Movement Envelope W0012346 to the Drug Receival Unit and handed it to Senior Constable Smith.

1. During the

In a police statement the ‘IDENTIFY’ marginal note should again be when the offender is categorically identified by name and not by description. This does not include the first instance the police associate the name with an offence. Once the suspect has been identified by name in the police statement he/she is to then be referred to as the accused thereafter.

Statement Conclusion

The conclusion of the statement should outline the final piece of information that witness has to offer the court. A witness statement may include the circumstances that caused the witness to supply a statement i.e. I then attend at the Wanneroo Police Station and gave this statement.

A Police Statement in which the investigator prefers a charge may need to state the final dealings they had with the accused i.e. At the conclusion of the interview, the present charge was preferred and whether the accused was arrested, charged and bailed, or was charged by summons. It is not necessary to state that the accused was lodged at the lock-up.

Investigators should be cautious as to how they conclude the statement and avoid terminology such as ‘the accused refused to participate in a video record of interview’. This type of statement has been deemed to be prejudicial to the accused as the jury may infer some guilt as to why an innocent person would refuse to talk to police.

sections.h:
The Criminal Procedures Act19 mandates that a declaration must be placed at the end of every statement that is made by a person who is twelve years of age or over.

The Criminal Procedures Act outlines that the declaration complies with the act if it contains words to the effect of:

This statement is true to the best of my knowledge and belief. I have made this statement knowing that, if it is tendered in evidence, I will be guilty of a crime if I have wilfully included in the statement anything that I know to be false or that I do not believe is true20.

To comply with this schedule of the act the WA Police have adopted the following as the standard jurat to be contained within a witness statement:

This statement is true to the best of my knowledge and belief. I have made this statement knowing that, if it is tendered in evidence, I will be guilty of a crime if I have wilfully included in the statement anything that I know to be false or that I do not believe is true.

Where the maker of a statement is a child of less than twelve years of age then the following declaration should be contained within the statement:

I know what the truth is and what lies are. Everything I have said today is the truth and is what happened to me. I have not told any lies. I know it is wrong to tell lies.

Where the statement is made by a person who cannot read, it should be read aloud to them by a person whom the witness nominates (not the interviewing officer), before they sign it.

It is accompanied by a declaration of the person who read the statement to the effect that it was so read.

The following declaration should be included after the official declaration:

I declare that this statement was read aloud to me whereupon I agree that its contents are true and correct and that the statement is in compliance with the above mentioned declaration.

19 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 (WA).

20 Criminal Procedure Act 2004 (WA), Schedule 3, s 4.

It is the accepted practice in Western Australia that the declaration forms the last paragraph of the statement. Ensure the declaration is presented in its entirety and not as a split paragraph.

Immediately following the declaration the maker of the statement and a witness must adopt the statement.

Adoption

Adoption is crucial for the legal admissibility of the statement and care in this area will ensure that the witness statement is not the subject of legal argument during the court case. The investigator must ensure that the person making the statement adopts it as being a true and accurate record of events.

Prior to the statement being adopted, the witness must be given the opportunity to read the statement and cause any amendment they wish to be made.

Every witness statement must be adopted by the maker and witnessed:

• For handwritten and typed hard copy statements the maker must sign and date the

bottom of every page observed by the witness, who then signs and dates the bottom of every page. On the last page further witness details such as where, time, date and who need to be completed.

• An electronic statement via the Statement Generator App can be completed with

electronic signatures appearing on the last page only.

Example of Required Details

Witness signature Police signature & No.

Time 12.30 pm Time 12.30 pm

Date 1st July 2004 Date 1st July 2004

Both comply with the Schedule 3 provisions of the Criminal Procedure Act 2004. The difference in adoption is due to the electronic statement initial pages are subject to PDF and cannot be changed whereas the hand written or typed hard copy initial pages can be amended and substituted if unsigned.

The final page of all statements must also include the suburb the statement was taken. If the witnessing officer’s name is not clearly evident from their signature, then they should legibly write their name along with their signature.

Final Page of Statement

Police Witness signature

Police Witness signature

It is most important that the officer taking a witness statement can identify the statement in court using their signature as a verification method. It is important an officer can demonstrate the witness was fully aware of the requirement to tell the truth during the interview and that the statement accurately represents their knowledge of the event(s). The correct adoption procedure and inclusion of the jurat are key methods of demonstrating the above.

Police statements are adopted in the same way as other witness statements and reinforce that a police witness is treated equally before the law.

At the completion of the statement the witness should be offered a copy of the statement as a matter of courtesy and professional conduct.

All copies of the statement including handwritten statements shall reflect the above mentioned adoption process.

Handwritten Statements

It is acceptable to take a handwritten statement from a witness. Handwritten statements are commonly taken at scenes of incidents. One common example is a victim's statement at the scene of an assault. The statement must be clearly legible.

Handwritten statements must comply with the Criminal Procedures Act to be considered acceptable in court21 and compliance with the details stated earlier in this subject will accomplish that.

21 Criminal Procedures Act 2004 (WA).

Do not use any type of correction fluid on handwritten statements, any mistakes or changes should have a neat line ruled through mistakes and initialed by the witness.

Where circumstances are such that only a handwritten statement can be obtained it should be retyped at the earliest possible opportunity and signed by the witness, remembering at all times retain the original handwritten statement, as it will form part of your case file and will be subject to disclosure.

All handwritten statements must also be copied onto the statement template for inclusion on a trial brief. The typed copy is to be attached to the handwritten statement.

Even though there is no legal need to have the typed copy signed by the witness, as a matter of professionalism it is recommended that investigators get all handwritten statements typed and signed, this will enhance the professionalism of the brief. The typed copy must be an exact copy of the handwritten statement in every detail, including any grammatical errors.

The details of the countersigning officer are also to be endorsed on the last page of the typed copy.

It is of utmost importance that witness statements are obtained as soon after the event as possible, the human memory is fallible and erodes with the passage of time22. The timely taking of a statement will aid in capturing the best evidence.

Versions of Statements

Planning an interview23 and gathering all the evidence a witness has to offer at the earliest opportunity is imperative. Poorly written first statements which omit salient points will necessitate additional work to capture the required information. This will then inevitably become a point of cross examination as to why such important information was not included in the first statement.

Where a witness makes a new statement, the original statement is to be attached to the rear of the new one. In most cases the contents of the first statement is generally repeated in the new one. However, by attaching the original statement to the new statement, the original statement date is still effective in relation to that evidence.

Where a witness is re-interviewed to clarify a matter, or provide further information relating to an event, the second statement should commence with:

‘Further to my statement made on (Date) I wish to provide further information that was not detailed in my previous statement’.

The statement should then detail what the additional information (observation) is and also explain why this was not included in the original statement.

Where additional information is supplied by a witness i.e. something they have done post the arrest, such as convey an item to Pathwest, then the additional or addendum statement is to be attached to the rear of the original statement.

22 See Investigative Interviewing Manual, Investigative Interviewing Philosophy and Theory. (Perth: WaPol 2017).

23 As above note 1.

Every subsequent or new statement is to be signed and dated when it was done, do not back date to the original statement date.

Where the subsequent or new statement conflicts with the original, the reason why should be stated by the witness in the subsequent or new statement.

Witnesses are also on occasion known to change their recollection of events, there can be a number of reasons for this to occur, but there is always the possibility that a witness may become hostile.

Hostile Witnesses

The Evidence Act deals with hostile witnesses and stipulates that the party producing the witness is not be allowed to impeach his credit by general evidence of bad character, but may contradict him by other evidence, if in the opinion of the judge he is hostile to the party producing him24.

The Judge may allow a previous inconsistent statement to be produced to the witness under certain circumstances25. A detailed statement taken at the time of the event can assist the prosecution in discrediting a hostile witness.

24 Evidence Act 1906 (WA), Section 20.

25 Evidence Act 1906 (WA), Section 22.

Negative Witness Statements

Occasions arise from time to time where the police interview a person who claims to have seen and heard nothing, knows nothing and will say nothing about an event , even though other evidence at hand may indicate otherwise.

There are various reasons why the witness may adopt this attitude. It may be because the witness entertains prejudices against the police generally, or it may be because the witness is aligned towards the suspect under investigation.

A statement should be obtained from the witness under these circumstances to the effect that the witness saw nothing, heard nothing, etc. Statements of this nature are referred to as ‘negative statements’, however this is a police term only and a negative statement should only ever be referred to as a statement.

In the event that the witness is called by defence counsel at a subsequent hearing, and offers evidence which is favourable to the accused, or in any way inconsistent with the previous statements made to the police investigator, the negative statement can be used by the prosecution to negate the credibility of the witness.

The taking of negative statements can be an integral part of the investigative process and should be considered at all times when a witness claims to know nothing but evidence suggests otherwise.

SUMMARY

Investigators should remember that witnesses are individuals, each may entertain a specific point of view, perception, bias or prejudice and from time to time a witness may even try to influence the course of an investigation by providing misleading information.

Some witnesses may be cooperative and others reluctant, some may be experts in a given field and others not, some witnesses may require special considerations to be afforded them either by law, established procedure or by circumstance e.g.: children, victims of sexual assaults, people who cannot speak or understand the English language.

The compilation of a witness statement is a crucial part of any investigation as it commences building a picture of what has happened. Attention to detail and thoroughness is essential when obtaining a witness statement, they should not be seen as mundane or insignificant, and a detailed witness statement has the potential to direct the investigation to its desired conclusion.

Witness statements also form an important part of the court process and a quality witness statement will reflect the professionalism of the investigator taking it and the professionalism of the investigation as a whole.

RELEVANT HEARSAY:

Hearsay evidence exceptions include, but are not limited to:

Admissions;

Evidence of early complaint;

Statements made through interpreters;

Dying Declarations;

Some declarations regarding documents; and

Certain statements made under ‘Res Gestae’, the principal of completeness.

Res gestae: the events, circumstances, remarks, etc. which relate to a particular case, especially as constituting admissible evidence in a court of law. RELEVANT HEARSAY:

Res gestae: the events, circumstances, remarks, etc. which relate to a particular case, especially as constituting admissible evidence in a court of law. In providing a brief to the ODPP or to Police Prosecutions, the objective must at all times, be to provide all relevant information pertinent to the allegations.

The issue of what represents hearsay evidence is a complex one and the admissibility of that evidence will often be the subject of legal argument.
